26: A fulfillment: 'After sixty-two weeks, an anointed one shall be cut off and shall have nothing.' The phrase 'cut off' (yikaret) is the judicial-death vocabulary of Torah (used for capital offenses). The anointed one is cut off not for his own sins (the grammar allows 'and there is nothing to him' or 'but not for himself') — the same pattern as Isaiah 53:8 ('cut off out of the land of the living ... for the transgression of my people'). Regardless of the precise calculation of the seventy weeks, the Christological core is the same: the anointed one (the Messiah) dies, is cut off, apparently without inheriting anything — and yet this death is the very mechanism by which the six goals of Daniel 9:24 are accomplished. The cross is Daniel's predicted event.
1: Darius the Mede becomes king — the empire has shifted from Babylon to Medo-Persia. The political transition is the occasion for Daniel's greatest prayer. The son of David who would rule forever (2 Samuel 7:13) has not yet come; the empires of the world rotate their dominion while YHWH's promised King waits in the prophetic horizon. The timing of Darius's reign frames Daniel's intercession: human kings rise and fall while the eternal King's coming is calculated in weeks.
2: Daniel understands from Jeremiah's prophecy (Jeremiah 25:11; Daniel 29:10) that the 70-year desolation of Jerusalem is nearly complete. The attention to divinely appointed time — that even exile has a fixed duration — is the foundation for the Seventy Weeks prophecy that follows: just as exile had its appointed 70 years, the Messiah's advent has its appointed 490. Galatians 4:4 captures the same logic: 'When the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son' — Christ arrives at the end of the divinely measured period.
3: Daniel turns to the Lord in fasting, sackcloth, and ashes — the posture of extreme penitential intercession. This prayer becomes the occasion for the greatest Christological prophecy in the OT (Daniel 9:24-27). The posture of self-abasement before God — the prophet identifying himself with the sins of his people — mirrors Christ's self-identification with human sin: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). The intercessor who prays in sackcloth anticipates the Intercessor who wears a crown of thorns.
4: Daniel addresses YHWH as 'great and awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love' (šōmēr habbĕrît wĕhahesed) — the same covenant vocabulary used in the Mosaic renewal formulas. The prayer's opening identifies the covenantal framework within which the 70 years must be understood, and within which the Messiah's coming will operate. Christ is the one who keeps the covenant perfectly on Israel's behalf: 'this is my blood of the covenant which is poured out for many' (Matthew 26:28) — the new covenant is YHWH's covenant-keeping fulfilled in his Son.
5: The confession accumulates verbs of sin: sinned, committed iniquity, done wickedly, rebelled, turned aside. Paul's similar accumulation in Romans 3:10-18 ('none righteous ... none who does good') draws from this same confessional vocabulary to establish universal human sinfulness as the premise for the gospel. The catalogue of human failure is the background against which Christ's obedience shines: 'by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous' (Romans 5:19) — the answer to every verb of Daniel's confession.
6: Israel has not listened to YHWH's servants the prophets who spoke in his name to every class of society. The prophets were rejected — and Jesus identifies himself as the culmination of this rejected prophetic line: 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you' (Matthew 23:37). The rejection of the prophets that Daniel confesses is the pattern that ends in the rejection of the Son: 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11).
7: To the Lord belongs righteousness; to Israel belongs shame in all the countries of their scattering. The contrast between divine righteousness and human shame is the theological baseline for justification. Christ takes the shame of the scattered exile-people upon himself (Hebrews 12:2: 'enduring the cross, despising the shame') and imputes to them the very righteousness that Daniel declares belongs to the Lord: 'the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe' (Romans 3:22).
8: Shame belongs to Israel's kings, princes, and fathers — the leadership class who bore responsibility for the covenant's maintenance. In Christ, the shame of the failed leadership is concentrated and absorbed: he is crucified between two criminals (Isaiah 53:12: 'numbered with the transgressors'), bearing the shame of all who betrayed the covenant. The reversal is complete at resurrection: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9).
9: 'To the Lord our God belong mercy and forgiveness, for we have rebelled against him.' The mercy (raḥamîm) and forgiveness (sĕliḥôt) that Daniel appeals to are not human achievements but divine attributes. Christ is the concrete form of this mercy and forgiveness: 'In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace' (Ephesians 1:7). What Daniel appeals to as divine attributes, Paul announces as accomplished facts in the person of Christ.
10: Israel has not obeyed the voice of YHWH or walked in his Torah as set before them through the prophets. The failure to walk in the Torah — the pattern repeated through generations — defines the need for the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34, in which the law is written on the heart. Christ fulfills both the law's demand (Matthew 5:17) and the new covenant's promise (Hebrews 8:10), himself the one who walked perfectly in the Torah and now enables his people to do the same through the Spirit (Romans 8:4).
11: 'The curse and oath written in the Law of Moses the servant of God have been poured out upon us, because we have sinned against him.' The curse of the law — systematically enumerated in Deuteronomy 28 — has fallen on the nation. Paul's Christology of the curse is the NT answer: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, “Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree”' (Galatians 3:13). The very curse Daniel confesses is the one Christ absorbs at the cross.
12: YHWH has confirmed his words against Jerusalem, bringing unprecedented disaster. The catastrophe of 586 BCE is the model for the judgment Jesus predicts for 70 CE (Matthew 24:21: 'great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world until now'). But in both cases — 586 and 70 CE — judgment serves a redemptive purpose: the refining of a remnant who will receive the new covenant. Christ weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) as Daniel prays over it, both knowing the catastrophe that faithlessness brings.
13: 'Yet we have not entreated the favor of the LORD our God, turning from our iniquities and paying attention to your truth.' The failure of repentance — Israel knows the judgment has come as written, yet does not seek the Lord's face. Jesus's call is precisely this turning: 'The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel' (Mark 1:15). His advent is the moment when the face of YHWH to be sought becomes the face of the Son incarnate.
14: 'The LORD has watched over the disaster and brought it upon us, for the LORD our God is righteous in all the works that he has done.' The theodicy of the exile: YHWH is righteous, not arbitrary. The same confession frames the cross: 'It was the will of the LORD to crush him; he has put him to grief' (Isaiah 53:10). The cross is the greatest concentration of divine justice in history — YHWH is righteous even there, perhaps especially there, where his righteousness is satisfied precisely through the crushing of his righteous Servant.
15: 'O Lord our God, who brought your people out of the land of Egypt with a mighty hand ... we have sinned, we have done wickedly.' The Exodus is the paradigm of divine redemption — the 'mighty hand' (yād ḥăzāqāh) is the standard Exodus idiom. Jesus's Transfiguration conversation is about his 'exodus' which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem (Luke 9:31). The greater Exodus — from sin and death rather than Egypt — is accomplished by the same God who stretched out his mighty hand, now incarnate in the Son who 'went out' to the cross.
16: 'O Lord, according to all your righteous acts, let your anger and your wrath turn away from your city Jerusalem, your holy mountain.' Daniel prays for the wrath to turn away from Jerusalem and its holy mountain. The wrath turns away from the city permanently in Christ: Hebrews 12:22-24 announces that believers have come 'to Mount Zion ... to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel.' The holy mountain's curse is lifted by the better blood.
17: 'Now therefore, O our God, listen to the prayer of your servant and to his pleas for mercy, and for your own sake, O Lord, make your face shine upon your sanctuary that is desolate.' The petition — 'make your face shine upon your sanctuary' — echoes the Aaronic benediction (Numbers 6:25: 'the LORD make his face shine on you'). The face of God shines most fully in Christ: '2 Corinthians 4:6 — God who said, “Let light shine out of darkness,” has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.'
18: 'We do not present our pleas before you because of our righteousness, but because of your great mercy.' The basis of prayer is not merit but mercy — the foundation of all access to God in both Testaments. In Christ, mercy has a concrete form: 'Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need' (Hebrews 4:16). The throne of grace is the same mercy-seat Daniel appeals to, now made accessible through the great high priest.
19: 'O Lord, hear; O Lord, forgive; O Lord, pay attention and act. Delay not, for your own sake, O my God, because your city and your people are called by your name.' The triple imperative of desperate prayer reaches its climax: 'Delay not!' This urgency — not for Daniel's sake but for the Lord's name — parallels the 'Thy kingdom come' of the Lord's Prayer and the 'Come, Lord Jesus' of Revelation 22:20. Christ himself in Gethsemane ('if it be possible, let this cup pass from me') and in Revelation 22:12 ('Behold, I am coming soon') inhabits both sides of this urgent prayer.
20: Daniel is speaking, praying, confessing sin, presenting his plea before YHWH his God 'for the holy mountain of my God.' The intercession is for the holy mountain — the place where God's name dwells. Jesus is the true holy mountain: Hebrews 12:22 ('you have come to Mount Zion') and John 4:23 ('the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father') together announce that the holy mountain of Daniel's prayer is fulfilled in the person of Christ, who draws all worshipers to himself.
21: 'While I was speaking in prayer, the man Gabriel, whom I had seen in the vision at the first, came to me in swift flight at the time of the evening sacrifice.' Gabriel — who later announces the birth of John the Baptist and the conception of Jesus (Luke 1:19-20, 26-38) — arrives at the time of the evening sacrifice. The evening offering is the tamid of Exodus 29:41. Gabriel's appearances form a unified arc: to Daniel to announce the Messiah's timetable, then to Zechariah and Mary to announce his birth. The angel who carries the prophecy of the Messiah carries the birth announcement of the same Messiah.
22: 'I have come to give you insight and understanding.' The angelic mission is epistemological: to give Daniel the ability to understand the divine plan. Jesus declares: 'The Father knows the Son and the Son knows the Father' (Matthew 11:27), and 'No one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.' Christ is the one in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Colossians 2:3) — the insight that Gabriel brings to Daniel is a foretaste of the full revelation of God's wisdom in Christ.
23: 'At the beginning of your pleas for mercy a word went out, and I have come to tell it to you, for you are greatly loved.' Daniel is addressed as ḥămûdôt (greatly loved, precious, desirable). The same word is used only a handful of times in the OT (cf. Daniel 10:11, 19). The 'greatly loved' address to Daniel prepares for the far greater 'beloved' addresses at Jesus's baptism ('This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased,' Matthew 3:17) and Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5). The prophet called 'greatly loved' receives the prophecy of the one who is supremely beloved.
24: The six purposes of the Seventy Weeks are a compressed Christological program: (1) 'to finish the transgression' — Christ's atoning death stops the accumulation of covenant-breaking; (2) 'to put an end to sin' — justification abolishes sin's condemning power; (3) 'to atone for iniquity' — propitiation through his blood (Romans 3:25); (4) 'to bring in everlasting righteousness' — the imputed righteousness of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:21); (5) 'to seal both vision and prophet' — the fulfillment of all OT prophecy in Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20: 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him'); (6) 'to anoint a most holy place' — Christ's baptismal anointing (Luke 4:18: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me') and the consecration of his body as the new temple. All six purposes are accomplished in Christ's first advent and its eschatological completion.
25: 'Know therefore and understand that from the going out of the word to restore and build Jerusalem to the coming of an anointed one, a prince, there shall be seven weeks. Then for sixty-two weeks it shall be built again with squares and moat, but in a troubled time.' The Messiah is called 'māšîaḥ nāgîd' (anointed prince/leader) — 'anointed' is the Hebrew source of both 'Messiah' and 'Christ.' The arithmetic points from Artaxerxes's decree (444 BCE, Nehemiah 2:1-8) across 483 years (7+62 weeks × 7) toward the first century, consistently associated with Jesus's public ministry. The 69 weeks end at the coming of the Anointed One — the very title Jesus accepts at his baptism (Acts 10:38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power').
27: 'And he shall make a strong covenant with many for one week, and for half of the week he shall put an end to sacrifice and offering.' The final week and the covenant confirmed with many: Jesus's three-and-a-half-year ministry culminates in the Last Supper ('this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many,' Matthew 26:28 — 'many' echoing Daniel 9:27's 'many'). In the middle of the week the sacrifice ends — at the cross, the veil of the temple is torn (Matthew 27:51), the sacrificial system is made obsolete, and Christ's one sacrifice replaces all others (Hebrews 10:12: 'when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God'). The 'abomination that makes desolate' in the second half of Daniel 9:27 is what Jesus cites in Mark 13:14 as the sign of coming judgment — connecting the 70th week's language to the end-time Antichrist figure.
